The Karan Gupta

Poet · Researcher · Builder · Founder

Vol. I  ·  No. 26  ·  Tuesday, February 24, 2026  ·  San Francisco, CA

HOME | WORK | POETRY | COMMONPLACE | ABOUT | CONTACT
The Poetry Archive

Language as Technology, Silence as Signal

Poems written across five years — about cities, displacement, attention, inheritance, and the strange tenderness of being young in a world that is always changing. SF Youth Poet Laureate 2024.

Full text view
Poems

Cartography of Belonging

2024 · Karan Gupta

I have learned to read my city by its absences—

the laundromat that became a juice bar,

the corner store where Mr. Kim's son

used to let me run a tab in summer.


Every neighborhood is a palimpsest.

Scratch the reclaimed wood and find

the Cantonese restaurant, the SSI office,

the church that fed everyone on Tuesdays.


We say displaced like weather,

like tectonic shift, like it was no one's fault.

The tectonics had venture capital.

The weather had a term sheet.


Still, I belong here,

even if here keeps moving.

I am the notation in the margin

of someone else's story about discovery.


I have learned to carry my city

like a folded map in my back pocket—

creased at all the wrong places,

true anyway.

Audio recording coming soon

I have learned to read my city by its absences—

the laundromat that became a juice bar,

the corner store where Mr. Kim's son

used to let me run a tab in summer.


Every neighborhood is a palimpsest.

Scratch the reclaimed wood and find

the Cantonese restaurant, the SSI office,

the church that fed everyone on Tuesdays.


We say displaced like weather,

like tectonic shift, like it was no one's fault.

The tectonics had venture capital.

The weather had a term sheet.


Still, I belong here,

even if here keeps moving.

I am the notation in the margin

of someone else's story about discovery.


I have learned to carry my city

like a folded map in my back pocket—

creased at all the wrong places,

true anyway.

First Generation

2024 · Karan Gupta

My mother doesn't understand

the word "burnout" but she understands

lying on the kitchen floor

too tired to move.


She calls it resting.

She calls it being human.

She makes tea.


I have two countries inside me

and neither one is whole.

In one, I am always arriving.

In the other, I never quite left.


My father taught me: work

is the longest sentence

and also the shortest prayer.


I am conjugating myself

into a language he invented

so I wouldn't need to.

Audio recording coming soon

My mother doesn't understand

the word "burnout" but she understands

lying on the kitchen floor

too tired to move.


She calls it resting.

She calls it being human.

She makes tea.


I have two countries inside me

and neither one is whole.

In one, I am always arriving.

In the other, I never quite left.


My father taught me: work

is the longest sentence

and also the shortest prayer.


I am conjugating myself

into a language he invented

so I wouldn't need to.

Notes on Attention

2023 · Karan Gupta

The algorithm knows what I want

before I have finished wanting it.


This is not magic.

This is a very large spreadsheet

of every moment I looked away.


I give my attention like charity—

freely and without thinking

about where it ends up.


Somewhere, a server hums.

It is keeping score.

It does not believe in rest.


I am trying to learn

how to want slowly.

How to sit with the not-knowing.

How to be bored

without immediately solving it.


The window is enough.

The light doing that thing.

The neighbor's dog.

This ordinary Tuesday,

attended to.

Audio recording coming soon

The algorithm knows what I want

before I have finished wanting it.


This is not magic.

This is a very large spreadsheet

of every moment I looked away.


I give my attention like charity—

freely and without thinking

about where it ends up.


Somewhere, a server hums.

It is keeping score.

It does not believe in rest.


I am trying to learn

how to want slowly.

How to sit with the not-knowing.

How to be bored

without immediately solving it.


The window is enough.

The light doing that thing.

The neighbor's dog.

This ordinary Tuesday,

attended to.

What the City Taught Me

2023 · Karan Gupta

The city taught me that beauty

is always beside something broken.


The city taught me to walk fast

and look up anyway.


The city taught me that everyone

is performing a version of themselves

for an audience that isn't watching.


The city taught me fog.

How it arrives without announcement.

How it is not absence but presence

of a different kind.


The city taught me that home

is the place where strangers

feel almost familiar.


I am still learning.

The city has more to say.

Audio recording coming soon

The city taught me that beauty

is always beside something broken.


The city taught me to walk fast

and look up anyway.


The city taught me that everyone

is performing a version of themselves

for an audience that isn't watching.


The city taught me fog.

How it arrives without announcement.

How it is not absence but presence

of a different kind.


The city taught me that home

is the place where strangers

feel almost familiar.


I am still learning.

The city has more to say.

Builder's Psalm

2024 · Karan Gupta

Praise the broken thing

that teaches its own repair.

Praise the child who finds

the fault line with her fingers.


Praise the kit that comes

without instructions.

Praise the hour of figuring it out.


I have watched a ten-year-old

light an LED for the first time—

that particular silence

before the knowledge becomes ordinary.


We forget that we learned everything.

Nothing arrived knowing itself.


Praise the mistake that teaches.

Praise the teacher who knows

when to leave the room.


This is the sacred work:

to hand someone a circuit

and step back

and wait

for the light.

Audio recording coming soon

Praise the broken thing

that teaches its own repair.

Praise the child who finds

the fault line with her fingers.


Praise the kit that comes

without instructions.

Praise the hour of figuring it out.


I have watched a ten-year-old

light an LED for the first time—

that particular silence

before the knowledge becomes ordinary.


We forget that we learned everything.

Nothing arrived knowing itself.


Praise the mistake that teaches.

Praise the teacher who knows

when to leave the room.


This is the sacred work:

to hand someone a circuit

and step back

and wait

for the light.

From the Commonplace Book

"The test of a first-rate intelligence is the ability to hold two opposed ideas in the mind at the same time, and still retain the ability to function."

— F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Crack-Up

I return to this constantly when I feel the tension between making art and making things that work. The capacity to hold contradiction without resolving it prematurely — this seems central to both poetic and engineering thinking.

"A city is not gauged by its length and width, but by the broadness of its vision and the height of its dreams."

— Herb Caen, Baghdad-by-the-Bay

Herb Caen was San Francisco's poet laureate before the role existed — his daily column the city's unofficial diary for forty years. I think about this when I watch the skyline change and wonder what we're dreaming now.

"Play is the highest form of research."

— Albert Einstein, Attributed

The whole premise of PlayKits. Also a useful antidote to the utilitarian pressure that follows every young person who shows aptitude for something. Not everything worth doing is worth monetizing.

"We are the stories we tell ourselves."

— Joan Didion, The White Album

And also the stories others tell about us, and the stories we refuse. Identity is narrative — partial, revised, subject to new evidence. I find this liberating rather than destabilizing.

"The brain is wider than the sky."

— Emily Dickinson, Poems (Fr598)

Working at Neuroscape, I keep this poem nearby. We map the brain with electrodes and fMRI and still the thing eludes us. Dickinson got there first with sixteen words.

"In the beginner's mind there are many possibilities, but in the expert's mind there are few."

— Shunryu Suzuki, Zen Mind, Beginner's Mind

The danger of competence: certainty calcifies into blindness. I try to carry beginners' mind into every new project — to ask the naive question that the experts stopped asking years ago.
